articles.title: Flight and Concealment: Surviving the Holocaust Underground in Munich and Beyond Susanna Schrafstetter, trans. Allison Brown
PRESERVING THROUGH INNOVATING: NEO-PASHKEVILS AND SYMBOLIC BOUNDARY WORK IN HAREDI ANTI-DRAFT PROTEST
articles.authors: Rosenberg, Hananel; Lowenstein-Barkai, Hila; Aharoni, Matan
articles.abstract: Abstract This study investigates the emergence of neo-pashkevils—visually and rhetorically modernized wall posters—within ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) campaigns opposing military conscription in Israel. While traditional pashkevils (wall posters) function as informal instruments of ideological enforcement and communal discipline, neo-pashkevils incorporate comic-style illustrations, vibrant colors, and colloquial language aimed at youth audiences. Through the lens of symbolic boundary work theory, the present study analyzes 42 posters from the “Hardakim” campaign against enlisting in the IDF (2013–2024), in order to examine how these hybrid forms represent not a rupture with tradition but a strategic recalibration that reinforces communal boundaries through esthetic and affective innovation. The findings identify three interrelated dimensions of boundary work—participatory performance, internal differentiation, and reflexive adaptation—through which neo-pashkevils mobilize stylistic tools to sustain collective identity against ideological threat. By extending boundary work theory to a highly stylized religious medium, the study sheds light on how conservative communities integrate modern media strategies to uphold ideological continuity, revealing the dynamic interplay between cultural preservation and communicative adaptation.

articles.title: 1956 AND “THE JEWISH QUESTION” IN HUNGARY
articles.abstract: Abstract The literature generally holds that “the Jewish Question” did not arise even tangentially during the 1956 Hungarian Uprising. We will examine the correctness of this assessment, focusing on Jewish participation in the uprising, (non-) antisemitism present during the events, and the components of the fear that arose among the Jews. We will seek to dispel, by means of contemporary documents and an extensive study of the literature, the common misconceptions that exist on many sides about the 1956 revolution in relation to the Jews.
